content_markdown:
A siren at rest emits a sound of frequency $f_0$. The speed of sound in air is $v$. The siren moves away from an observer at rest relative to the siren in a straight line with constant speed $v_s$.

![diagram]

The observer measures a frequency lower than $f_0$ because the

diagram_metadata.description: A diagram showing an observer standing on the left and a car (siren) moving to the right away from the observer with a velocity vector labeled v_s.

options.label: A
options.text: speed at which the sound moves relative to the observer is $v - v_s$.

options.label: B
options.text: speed at which the sound moves relative to the observer is $v_s - v$.

options.label: C
options.text: wavelength measured by the observer is smaller by a factor $\frac{v_s}{v}$.

options.label: D
options.text: wavelength measured by the observer is greater by a factor $\frac{v_s}{v}$.

vibe_explanation: In the Doppler effect for a moving source, the speed of sound in the medium (relative to the observer) remains constant at $v$. The change in frequency is caused by the source 'stretching' or 'compressing' the wavefronts in space. When the source moves away, each successive crest is emitted from a position further away than the previous one, resulting in an increased wavelength $\lambda' = \lambda + \Delta \lambda$, where $\Delta \lambda = v_s T = \frac{v_s}{f_0}$. This makes the observed wavelength greater.
